question: Find the first name and gender of student who have more than one pet.

question: Find the last name of the student who has a cat that is age 3.

FROM Student
INNER JOIN Has_Pet ON Student.StuID = Has_Pet.StuID

question: Which countries in europe have at least 3 car manufacturers?

joins: countries JOIN continents ON countries.Continent = continents.ContId
car_makers JOIN countries ON car_makers.Country = countries.CountryId

notes: Use LOWER() for case-insensitive continent comparison to handle potential case variations.

Count car_makers.Id to count manufacturers per country.
Group by CountryName to aggregate manufacturers per country.
No need to join model_list, car_names, or cars_data — not relevant to the question.

final_sql:
SELECT countries.CountryName
FROM continents
INNER JOIN countries ON countries.Continent = continents.ContId
INNER JOIN car_makers ON car_makers.Country = countries.CountryId
WHERE LOWER(continents.Continent) = LOWER('Europe')
GROUP BY countries.CountryName
HAVING COUNT(car_makers.Id) >= 3;

where: cars_data.Accelerate > (SELECT cars_data.Accelerate FROM cars_data ORDER BY CAST(cars_data.Horsepower AS REAL) DESC LIMIT 1)
notes: Horsepower is stored as TEXT, so CAST to REAL for proper numeric comparison.
The subquery finds the Accelerate value of the car with max Horsepower.
Outer query counts all cars with Accelerate greater than that value.
No joins needed — all data is in cars_data.
No grouping or distinct needed — simple count of rows meeting condition.

final_sql: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM cars_data WHERE cars_data.Accelerate > (SELECT cars_data.Accelerate FROM cars_data ORDER BY CAST(cars_data.Horsepower AS REAL) DESC LIMIT 1);

question: How many countries has more than 2 car makers ?

joins: car_makers JOIN countries ON car_makers.Country = countries.CountryId

reasoner_decision.intent: Count the number of countries that have more than 2 car makers.
notes: Join car_makers and countries via Country (FK) to CountryId (PK).
Group by countries.CountryId to count car makers per country.
Use HAVING to filter groups with more than 2 car makers.
Count the resulting countries with COUNT(*).
No need for additional tables or columns; schema constraints are sufficient.

final_sql: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM car_makers JOIN countries ON car_makers.Country = countries.CountryId GROUP BY countries.CountryId HAVING COUNT(car_makers.Id) > 2;

question: What is the number of countries with more than 2 car makers ?

Group by countries.CountryId to count car makers per country.
Use HAVING to filter groups with more than 2 car makers.

question: For the cars with 4 cylinders, which model has the largest horsepower?

reasoner_decision.intent: Find the model with the largest horsepower among cars having 4 cylinders.
notes: Horsepower is stored as TEXT; implicit conversion to numeric is assumed for ordering.
Model is retrieved from model_list as required by the question.
Join path follows FK links: cars_data -> car_names -> model_list.
No grouping needed since we are finding a single maximum.
LIMIT 1 with DESC ordering correctly retrieves the model with highest horsepower.

final_sql: SELECT model_list.Model FROM cars_data INNER JOIN car_names ON cars_data.Id = car_names.MakeId INNER JOIN model_list ON car_names.Model = model_list.Model WHERE cars_data.Cylinders = 4 ORDER BY cars_data.Horsepower DESC LIMIT 1;

question: For all of the 4 cylinder cars, which model has the most horsepower?

joins: cars_data JOIN car_names ON cars_data.Id = car_names.MakeId
car_names JOIN model_list ON car_names.Model = model_list.Model
model_list JOIN car_makers ON model_list.Maker = car_makers.Id

reasoner_decision.intent: Find the model with the highest horsepower among 4-cylinder cars.
notes: Horsepower is stored as TEXT; implicit conversion assumed in SQL engine.
Only car_names.Model is needed in SELECT as the question asks for the model.
cars_data and car_names are sufficient; other tables (model_list, car_makers) are unused.
Join condition uses PK-FK link: cars_data.Id → car_names.MakeId.
No grouping needed since MAX and LIMIT 1 suffice for extremum query.

final_sql: SELECT car_names.Model FROM cars_data JOIN car_names ON cars_data.Id = car_names.MakeId WHERE cars_data.Cylinders = 4 ORDER BY cars_data.Horsepower DESC LIMIT 1;

question: Among the cars with more than lowest horsepower, which ones do not have more than 3 cylinders? List the car makeid and make name.

reasoner_decision.intent: Find car makes (makeid and make name) of cars with horsepower above the minimum but with 3 or fewer cylinders.
where: CAST(cars_data.Horsepower AS INTEGER) > (SELECT MIN(CAST(Horsepower AS INTEGER)) FROM cars_data)
notes: Horsepower is stored as TEXT, so CAST to INTEGER is required for comparison.
car_names.Make references car_makers.Id (not Maker), per schema FK link.
Only tables with used columns are included: car_makers, cars_data, car_names.
No grouping or aggregation needed; filtering suffices.
Ensure string literals in WHERE are case-insensitive if needed, but numeric comparisons are safe.

final_sql:
SELECT car_makers.Id, car_makers.Maker
FROM car_makers
INNER JOIN car_names ON car_names.MakeId = cars_data.Id
INNER JOIN cars_data ON car_names.Make = car_makers.Id
WHERE CAST(cars_data.Horsepower AS INTEGER) > (SELECT MIN(CAST(Horsepower AS INTEGER)) FROM cars_data)
  AND cars_data.Cylinders <= 3;

Join car_makers to car_names using Make = Id (FK-PK).

INNER JOIN car_names ON cars_data.Id = car_names.MakeId

question: Which models are lighter than 3500 but not built by the 'Ford Motor Company'?

joins: cars_data JOIN car_names ON cars_data.Id = car_names.MakeId
car_names JOIN model_list ON car_names.Model = model_list.Model
model_list JOIN car_makers ON model_list.Maker = car_makers.Id

reasoner_decision.intent: Find car models that weigh less than 3500 and are not manufactured by Ford Motor Company.
notes: Weight is numeric and compared directly with 3500.
FullName must exactly match 'Ford Motor Company' as given.
Model is selected from model_list as per question.
Joins follow FK relationships: cars_data -> car_names -> model_list -> car_makers.
No aggregation or grouping needed; simple filtering suffices.

final_sql:
SELECT model_list.Model
FROM cars_data
INNER JOIN car_names ON cars_data.Id = car_names.MakeId
INNER JOIN model_list ON car_names.Model = model_list.Model
INNER JOIN car_makers ON model_list.Maker = car_makers.Id
WHERE cars_data.Weight < 3500
AND car_makers.FullName != 'Ford Motor Company';

aggregations: GROUP BY car_makers.Id, car_makers.Maker HAVING COUNT(model_list.ModelId) >= 2

question: What are the ids and makers of all car makers that produce at least 2 models and make more than 3 cars?

joins: car_makers JOIN model_list ON car_makers.Id = model_list.Maker
model_list JOIN cars_data ON model_list.ModelId = cars_data.Id

Join model_list to cars_data via ModelId = Id (FK)
Count models using model_list.ModelId and cars using cars_data.Id
Group by maker ID and name to aggregate per maker
HAVING ensures at least 2 models and more than 3 cars

question: What are the id and names of the countries which have more than 3 car makers or produce the 'fiat' model?

joins: countries JOIN car_makers ON countries.CountryId = car_makers.Country
car_makers JOIN model_list ON car_makers.Id = model_list.Maker

reasoner_decision.intent: Find countries with more than 3 car makers or that produce the 'fiat' model, returning their id and name.
notes: Use LOWER() for case-insensitive match on 'fiat' to handle potential case variations.
Join path is valid: countries → car_makers → model_list via FKs.
GROUP BY and HAVING correctly aggregate car makers per country.
SELECT includes required columns: CountryId and CountryName.
No need for DISTINCT as grouping ensures unique countries.

final_sql:
SELECT countries.CountryId, countries.CountryName
FROM countries
JOIN car_makers ON countries.CountryId = car_makers.Country
JOIN model_list ON car_makers.Id = model_list.Maker
WHERE LOWER(model_list.Model) = LOWER('fiat')
GROUP BY countries.CountryId, countries.CountryName
HAVING COUNT(car_makers.Id) > 3;

car_makers JOIN model_list ON car_makers.Id = model_list.Maker

question: What is the name of the visitor who visited both a museum opened before 2009 and a museum opened after 2011?

reasoner_decision.intent: Find visitors who visited at least one museum opened before 2009 and at least one museum opened after 2011.
where: visitor.ID IN (SELECT visit.visitor_ID FROM visit JOIN museum ON visit.Museum_ID = museum.Museum_ID WHERE museum.Open_Year < '2009')
visitor.ID IN (SELECT visit.visitor_ID FROM visit JOIN museum ON visit.Museum_ID = museum.Museum_ID WHERE museum.Open_Year > '2011')
notes: Planner incorrectly uses AND on Open_Year < '2009' and Open_Year > '2011' — impossible in one row.
Need to find visitors who satisfy both conditions across different visits.
Use subqueries to identify visitor IDs meeting each condition separately.
Join museum and visit only in subqueries; main query filters visitor by ID.
Ensure Name is selected from visitor table as requested.

final_sql:
SELECT DISTINCT visitor.Name
FROM visitor
WHERE visitor.ID IN (
    SELECT visit.visitor_ID
    FROM visit
    JOIN museum ON visit.Museum_ID = museum.Museum_ID
    WHERE museum.Open_Year < '2009'
)
AND visitor.ID IN (
    SELECT visit.visitor_ID
    FROM visit
    JOIN museum ON visit.Museum_ID = museum.Museum_ID
    WHERE museum.Open_Year > '2011'
);

FROM players
INNER JOIN matches ON matches.winner_id = players.player_id
WHERE matches.year = 2013
INTERSECT

FROM players
INNER JOIN matches ON matches.winner_id = players.player_id

joins: players JOIN matches ON players.player_id = matches.winner_id

having: SUM(CASE WHEN matches.tourney_name = 'WTA Championships' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) > 0
SUM(CASE WHEN matches.tourney_name = 'Australian Open' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) > 0
notes: Condition 'tourney_name = 'WTA Championships' AND tourney_name = 'Australian Open'' is logically impossible; must use aggregation to find players winning both.
Use GROUP BY on player to count wins across tournaments.
HAVING clause ensures player won both tournaments.
Join is correct via winner_id to players.player_id.
No need for DISTINCT since grouping by player ensures uniqueness.

SELECT players.country_code, players.first_name
FROM players
JOIN matches ON players.player_id = matches.winner_id
GROUP BY players.player_id, players.country_code, players.first_name
HAVING SUM(CASE WHEN matches.tourney_name = 'WTA Championships' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) > 0
   AND SUM(CASE WHEN matches.tourney_name = 'Australian Open' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) > 0;

question: How many battles did not lose any ship with tonnage '225'?

reasoner_decision.intent: Count battles where no ship lost had tonnage '225' — i.e., battles in which all lost ships had tonnage ≠ '225'.
notes: The planner plan incorrectly counts battles with at least one non-225 tonnage ship, not battles with NO 225-tonnage ship lost.
Correct logic: Find battles where NOT EXISTS any ship with tonnage='225' lost in that battle.
Current WHERE clause filters out ships with tonnage='225', but still includes battles that have both 225 and non-225 ships — wrong.
Need to use NOT EXISTS or anti-join pattern to exclude battles that have ANY ship with tonnage='225'.
Fix: Use subquery to exclude battle.id where ship.tonnage='225' exists.

final_sql:
SELECT COUNT(battle.id)
FROM battle
WHERE NOT EXISTS (
    SELECT 1
    FROM ship
    WHERE ship.lost_in_battle = battle.id
    AND ship.tonnage = '225'
);

question: Show names, results and bulgarian commanders of the battles with no ships lost in the 'English Channel'.

reasoner_decision.intent: Find battles in the English Channel with no ships lost, returning battle name, result, and Bulgarian commander.
where: battle.id NOT IN (SELECT ship.lost_in_battle FROM ship WHERE ship.location = 'English Channel')
EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM ship WHERE ship.location = 'English Channel' AND ship.lost_in_battle = battle.id)
notes: ship.lost_in_battle IS NULL is incorrect logic — we need battles where NO ship was lost, not ships with NULL loss.
Condition ship.location = 'English Channel' must be applied to ships associated with the battle, not battles directly.
Planner incorrectly joins ship to battle and filters ship.lost_in_battle IS NULL — this finds ships not in any battle, not battles with no ships lost.
Correct approach: exclude battles that have any ship lost in the English Channel.
No need to join ship table in FROM; use subquery to filter battles based on ship loss condition.

final_sql:
SELECT battle.name, battle.result, battle.bulgarian_commander
FROM battle
WHERE battle.id NOT IN (
    SELECT ship.lost_in_battle
    FROM ship
    WHERE ship.location = 'English Channel'
    AND ship.lost_in_battle IS NOT NULL
);

Students LEFT JOIN Student_Enrolment ON Students.student_id = Student_Enrolment.student_id

question: Find the semester when both Master students and Bachelor students got enrolled in.

joins: Student_Enrolment JOIN Degree_Programs ON Student_Enrolment.degree_program_id = Degree_Programs.degree_program_id
Student_Enrolment JOIN Semesters ON Student_Enrolment.semester_id = Semesters.semester_id

reasoner_decision.intent: Find semesters where both Master and Bachelor students were enrolled, using the same semester_id.
where: Semesters.semester_id IN (SELECT se1.semester_id FROM Student_Enrolment se1 JOIN Degree_Programs dp1 ON se1.degree_program_id = dp1.degree_program_id WHERE dp1.degree_summary_name = 'Master')
Semesters.semester_id IN (SELECT se2.semester_id FROM Student_Enrolment se2 JOIN Degree_Programs dp2 ON se2.degree_program_id = dp2.degree_program_id WHERE dp2.degree_summary_name = 'Bachelor')
notes: Original plan incorrectly uses AND on mutually exclusive degree_summary_name conditions; must use subqueries to find intersection.
Semester names are requested, so select Semesters.semester_name.
Join Student_Enrolment with Degree_Programs to filter by degree type.
No need to join with other tables; all needed data is in Student_Enrolment, Degree_Programs, and Semesters.
Use IN with subqueries to ensure semester has both Master and Bachelor enrolments.

final_sql:
SELECT Semesters.semester_name
FROM Semesters
WHERE Semesters.semester_id IN (
    SELECT se1.semester_id
    FROM Student_Enrolment se1
    JOIN Degree_Programs dp1 ON se1.degree_program_id = dp1.degree_program_id
    WHERE dp1.degree_summary_name = 'Master'
)
AND Semesters.semester_id IN (
    SELECT se2.semester_id
    FROM Student_Enrolment se2
    JOIN Degree_Programs dp2 ON se2.degree_program_id = dp2.degree_program_id
    WHERE dp2.degree_summary_name = 'Bachelor'
);

question: What is the id of the semester that had both Masters and Bachelors students enrolled?

joins: Semesters JOIN Student_Enrolment ON Semesters.semester_id = Student_Enrolment.semester_id
Student_Enrolment JOIN Degree_Programs ON Student_Enrolment.degree_program_id = Degree_Programs.degree_program_id

reasoner_decision.intent: Find the semester_id that has at least one student enrolled in a Masters program and at least one student enrolled in a Bachelors program.
having: SUM(CASE WHEN Degree_Programs.degree_summary_name = 'Masters' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) > 0
SUM(CASE WHEN Degree_Programs.degree_summary_name = 'Bachelors' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) > 0
notes: The planner incorrectly uses two ANDed equality conditions on degree_summary_name, which is logically impossible.
Must use HAVING with conditional aggregation to find semesters with both degree types.
Only Semesters, Student_Enrolment, and Degree_Programs are needed; Students and other tables are unused.
degree_summary_name values are literal strings 'Masters' and 'Bachelors' — match case exactly.
No DISTINCT needed; grouping by semester_id ensures uniqueness.

final_sql:
SELECT Semesters.semester_id
FROM Semesters
INNER JOIN Student_Enrolment ON Semesters.semester_id = Student_Enrolment.semester_id
INNER JOIN Degree_Programs ON Student_Enrolment.degree_program_id = Degree_Programs.degree_program_id
GROUP BY Semesters.semester_id
HAVING SUM(CASE WHEN Degree_Programs.degree_summary_name = 'Masters' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) > 0
   AND SUM(CASE WHEN Degree_Programs.degree_summary_name = 'Bachelors' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) > 0;

question: Which language is spoken by the largest number of countries?

joins: countrylanguage JOIN country ON countrylanguage.CountryCode = country.Code
aggregations: COUNT(countrylanguage.CountryCode) AS country_count GROUP BY countrylanguage.Language

reasoner_decision.intent: Find the language spoken by the largest number of countries, based on count of countries per language.
notes: Use COUNT(countrylanguage.CountryCode) to count countries per language.
Join countrylanguage with country via CountryCode = Code to ensure valid country references.
Group by Language to aggregate counts per language.
Order by count descending and limit to 1 to get the most spoken language.
No need to select country columns; only Language is requested.

final_sql:
SELECT countrylanguage.Language
FROM countrylanguage
JOIN country ON countrylanguage.CountryCode = country.Code
GROUP BY countrylanguage.Language
ORDER BY COUNT(countrylanguage.CountryCode) DESC
LIMIT 1;

question: What is the language spoken by the largest percentage of people in each country?

Group by country.Name to get one result per country.

question: What are the country codes of the different countries, and what are the languages spoken by the greatest percentage of people for each?

reasoner_decision.intent: Find the country code and the language spoken by the greatest percentage of people in each country.
notes: GROUP BY must include only country.Code since we want the top language per country.
SELECTing countrylanguage.Language without grouping by it is invalid unless aggregated; use subquery or window function.
Current plan incorrectly groups by Percentage and Language, which breaks the intent of finding the max per country.
To get the language with max percentage per country, use a subquery or ROW_NUMBER() window function.
Contract must be revised to use a window function or correlated subquery to find max Percentage per country.

final_sql:
SELECT c.Code, cl.Language
FROM country c
JOIN countrylanguage cl ON c.Code = cl.CountryCode
WHERE cl.Percentage = (
    SELECT MAX(cl2.Percentage)
    FROM countrylanguage cl2
    WHERE cl2.CountryCode = c.Code
);

where: Owners.owner_id NOT IN (SELECT Dogs.owner_id FROM Dogs WHERE Dogs.owner_id IS NOT NULL)
notes: The planner's condition 'Dogs.owner_id IS NULL' is incorrect for finding owners without dogs; it checks for null owner_id in Dogs, not missing owners.
Use NOT IN subquery to find owners whose owner_id does not appear in Dogs.owner_id.
Dogs.owner_id can be NULL only if dog has no owner, but we want owners with zero dogs — so exclude all owner_ids present in Dogs.
Avoid JOIN with condition IS NULL for this logic; LEFT JOIN + IS NULL is also valid but subquery is clearer here.
COUNT(Owners.owner_id) is correct since we count owner records meeting the condition.

final_sql: SELECT COUNT(Owners.owner_id) FROM Owners WHERE Owners.owner_id NOT IN (SELECT Dogs.owner_id FROM Dogs WHERE Dogs.owner_id IS NOT NULL);

question: Find the distinct breed type and size type combinations for dogs.

joins: Dogs JOIN Breeds ON Dogs.breed_code = Breeds.breed_code
Dogs JOIN Sizes ON Dogs.size_code = Sizes.size_code

reasoner_decision.intent: Find distinct breed type and size type combinations for dogs.
notes: Use DISTINCT to ensure unique breed-size combinations.
Breed and size codes are linked via Dogs table via FKs.
No filtering conditions needed; all dog records are relevant.
Grouping by breed_code and size_code ensures unique combinations.
No aggregation functions required beyond DISTINCT and GROUP BY.

final_sql:
SELECT DISTINCT Breeds.breed_code, Sizes.size_code
FROM Dogs
JOIN Breeds ON Dogs.breed_code = Breeds.breed_code
JOIN Sizes ON Dogs.size_code = Sizes.size_code
GROUP BY Breeds.breed_code, Sizes.size_code;

joins: Properties JOIN Ref_Property_Types ON Properties.property_type_code = Ref_Property_Types.property_type_code

reasoner_decision.intent: Retrieve property type descriptions for properties, joining Properties with Ref_Property_Types via property_type_code.
notes: Only Ref_Property_Types and Properties are needed as per schema and plan.
Join is correctly based on FK/PK link: Properties.property_type_code → Ref_Property_Types.property_type_code.
No filtering conditions or aggregations are required per question.
No ordering or limiting specified in question.
No distinct needed as question does not ask for unique descriptions.

final_sql: SELECT Ref_Property_Types.property_type_description FROM Properties INNER JOIN Ref_Property_Types ON Properties.property_type_code = Ref_Property_Types.property_type_code;

question: What are the names of properties that are either houses or apartments with more than 1 room?

joins: Properties JOIN Ref_Property_Types ON Properties.property_type_code = Ref_Property_Types.property_type_code

reasoner_decision.intent: Find property names that are houses or apartments with more than one room.
notes: Use LOWER() for case-insensitive matching of property type descriptions.
Only Properties and Ref_Property_Types are needed; no other tables or joins required.
room_count is numeric, so direct comparison > 1 is valid.
property_name is the only requested attribute; no aggregation needed.
No distinct required as question does not specify unique properties.

final_sql:
SELECT Properties.property_name
FROM Properties
JOIN Ref_Property_Types ON Properties.property_type_code = Ref_Property_Types.property_type_code
WHERE LOWER(Ref_Property_Types.property_type_description) IN ('house', 'apartment')
  AND Properties.room_count > 1;
